prompt:
Data-platform ticket. Our telemetry archiver pushes nightly rollup blobs to S3 with a single put_object call, and last night a 3.4 GB rollup timed out four times and the job gave up — we resend the whole object every time anything hiccups. I want a proper multipart uploader package, `s3mpu`, built on the official AWS SDK for Python (boto3 is pinned in requirements.txt; the venv setup is `python3 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt`). The uploader must take an injected S3 client (we construct clients ourselves with our own credentials/config plumbing — never create one inside the library).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official AWS documentation — at minimum the S3 CompleteMultipartUpload API reference (part ordering rules, special errors, multipart ETag shape) and the S3 multipart upload overview in the user guide (size threshold guidance, part number limits, abort semantics), plus the boto3 reference for the client methods you drive. Only first-party AWS pages count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Shape of the package (package `s3mpu/`, split across a few modules sensibly — planning, upload/retry, and errors shouldn't all live in one file):

- Module constants pinned to the documented values: MIN_PART_SIZE, DEFAULT_MULTIPART_THRESHOLD, MAX_PARTS, and RETRYABLE_ERROR_CODES (the set of S3/SDK error codes worth retrying — read the boto3 retry guide and docs/contract.json; terminal codes like AccessDenied or InvalidPart must not be in it).
- select_strategy(total_size, threshold=DEFAULT_MULTIPART_THRESHOLD) -> "single" | "multipart" — multipart at or above the threshold.
- plan_parts(total_size, part_size, *, min_part_size=MIN_PART_SIZE, max_parts=MAX_PARTS) -> ordered list of part plans, each with .number/.offset/.size — numbering starts at 1 and is consecutive (the docs explain why that matters once checksums are involved), last part carries the remainder, and it must raise ValueError for an empty object, a part_size below the floor, or a plan that would exceed the documented part-count maximum.
- MultipartUploader(client, bucket, *, part_size=..., threshold=..., min_part_size=..., max_attempts=4, base_delay=0.5, max_delay=8.0, sleep=time.sleep) with .threshold/.min_part_size attributes, and .upload(key, data: bytes, content_type=None) -> result.
- Below the threshold: one put_object with the checksum algorithm from docs/contract.json; at/above: create_multipart_upload, then upload_part for every planned part strictly in ascending part order, then complete_multipart_upload with the exact per-part fields the docs require, in ascending PartNumber order. ContentType is forwarded only when the caller provided one.
- Retries: a call that fails with a retryable error code is retried up to max_attempts total attempts, sleeping min(max_delay, base_delay * 2**(attempt-1)) between tries via the injected sleep. Terminal error codes fail immediately — no retry, no sleep.
- Failure handling: once a multipart upload has an UploadId and the upload cannot finish (retries exhausted or terminal error, including a failed complete), abort the multipart upload so we stop paying for orphaned parts, then raise s3mpu.UploadFailed carrying .key, .upload_id, .error_code (the S3 error code that killed it) with the original ClientError as __cause__. If the abort itself blows up, the original failure still wins.
- The result object surfaces what S3 returned: .strategy, .upload_id (None for single put), .etag, .checksum_crc32, and .parts — each with .number/.etag/.checksum_crc32 exactly as the service reported them.

Acceptance is test_uploader.py — it wraps a real boto3 client in botocore's Stubber, so every request is validated against the genuine S3 service model with dummy credentials and zero network. Run: bash run_verify.sh — until green. test_uploader.py, run_verify.sh, requirements.txt and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: S3 REST API 2006-03-01 multipart upload operations via AWS SDK for Python (boto3) 1.43.50

official_sources.title: CompleteMultipartUpload - Amazon Simple Storage Service
official_sources.used_for: completion envelope of PartNumber+ETag per part, parts concatenated in ascending part-number order, special errors EntityTooSmall/InvalidPart/InvalidPartOrder (400) and NoSuchUpload (404), multipart-object ETag ending in -<part count>, and checksum fields in the response

official_sources.title: Uploading and copying objects using multipart upload in Amazon S3 - Amazon Simple Storage Service
official_sources.used_for: 100 MB best-practice threshold for switching to multipart, part numbers 1-10,000, recording PartNumber+ETag from each UploadPart response for completion, composite checksums requiring consecutive part numbers starting at 1, and the requirement to complete or abort so stored parts stop accruing charges

official_sources.title: upload_part - Boto3 documentation
official_sources.used_for: boto3 S3 client parameter names (Body, Bucket, Key, PartNumber, UploadId, ChecksumAlgorithm) and the ETag/ChecksumCRC32 response fields the uploader must surface

official_sources.title: Retries - Boto3 documentation
official_sources.used_for: retryable error codes in the SDK's standard retry mode (RequestTimeout, SlowDown, Throttling, ThrottlingException, transient 5xx) and total_max_attempts semantics used by the harness to disable SDK-internal retries

research.language_rationale: boto3 is AWS's official Python SDK and the credible S3 integration surface in Python; the uploader drives an injected boto3 S3 client, and the acceptance tests pin the wire contract with botocore's Stubber, which validates each request against the real S3 service model.
